_note: This file covers the fundamental requirements for protecting cables against overload and short-circuit currents. The central requirement is the Fundamental Rule: Ib ≤ In ≤ Iz. Short-circuit protection requirements — including the adiabatic check — are in fault-current.json.

fundamental_rule._title: The Fundamental Rule of overcurrent protection

Ib.definition: Design current of the circuit — the maximum normal sustained load current
Ib.note: Ib is determined by the designer from the connected load, applying diversity. For motors, Ib is the full-load current. For heating, Ib is the rated current. For mixed loads, use diversity per local practice or IEC 60364-3.

In.definition: Nominal rated current of the protective device (MCB, fuse, RCBO)
In.note: In must be ≥ Ib (device must not nuisance-trip in normal use). In must also be ≤ Iz (device must protect the cable). Standard IEC sizes: 6, 10, 16, 20, 25, 32, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125, 160, 200, 250, 315, 400, 500, 630 A.

Iz.definition: Continuous current-carrying capacity of the cable, after correction factors
Iz.note: Iz = It × Ca × Cg × Ci × Cs × Cd. All applicable correction factors must be applied to the tabulated value (It) from Annex B of IEC 60364-5-52. The cable is the limiting element.

_where.I2: Effective operating current of the device — the current at which it WILL trip within conventional time
_where.I2_for_gG_fuse_IEC_60269: 1.6 × In (therefore for fuses: In ≤ Iz / 1.1 to satisfy I2 ≤ 1.45 × Iz)

additional_condition_for_effective_overl.implication_for_fuses: When protecting with gG fuses, the nominal fuse rating In must be ≤ 0.91 × Iz (or conservatively ≤ 0.9 × Iz) to satisfy the I2 ≤ 1.45 × Iz condition, because I2 = 1.6 × In for fuses.

overload_protection.requirement: Every cable must be protected against overload at or before the origin of the cable (or at a point where the current-carrying capacity changes).

position_of_protective_device.standard_position: At the origin of every circuit — at the point where the cable cross-section decreases (increases in impedance).

omission_permitted.condition_1: No branch circuit or outlet is present between the upstream device and the downstream device — i.e., the load is fixed and identifiable
omission_permitted.condition_2: The cable is protected against short-circuit at the upstream device and the cable supplies a fixed load that cannot be overloaded
omission_permitted._example: A 4mm² cable supplying a fixed 2kW heater, protected by a 16A MCB upstream — the heater cannot draw more than Ib = 2000/230 = 8.7A, so overload is physically impossible

MCB_IEC_60898.I1_non_tripping_current: 1.13 × In — must NOT trip within 1 hour (cold)

gG_fuse_IEC_60269.I1_non_tripping_current: 1.25 × In — must NOT melt within 1 hour

short_circuit_protection.requirement: Every protective device must be capable of interrupting any short-circuit current that can occur at its point of installation without sustaining damage to the installation.

breaking_capacity.definition: The maximum prospective short-circuit current (PSCC) the device can safely interrupt.
breaking_capacity.requirement: Device breaking capacity ≥ PSCC at the point of installation.

standard_MCB_breaking_capacities.3kA: Domestic — only where PSCC is confirmed ≤ 3kA (very long supply paths, high supply impedance)
standard_MCB_breaking_capacities.6kA: Most domestic and light commercial applications in many countries
standard_MCB_breaking_capacities.10kA: Commercial and industrial applications — standard specification in many markets
standard_MCB_breaking_capacities.16kA: Near main switchboards, high fault level environments
standard_MCB_breaking_capacities.25kA+: Industrial near transformers — use MCCBs or HRC fuses

cable_protection_against_short_circuit.requirement: The device must clear the fault before the cable is thermally damaged.
cable_protection_against_short_circuit.note: This is the adiabatic equation. All values are in fault-current.json.

back_up_protection.principle: Where a device does not have sufficient breaking capacity on its own, it may be backed up by a device with higher breaking capacity upstream, provided the combination is coordinated (the downstream device limits current let-through to within the upstream device's rating under all fault conditions).
back_up_protection.common_application: 6kA MCB backed up by 16kA MCCB at distribution board. This is only valid where the manufacturer's coordination data confirms the combination.
back_up_protection.warning: Do not assume any combination is valid without manufacturer coordination data.

MCB_Type_B.Ia_for_worst_case_Zs: 5× In (upper limit — use this for Zs_max calculation)
MCB_Type_B.typical_use: Resistive and mixed loads — general lighting, heating, socket outlets. Minimal inrush current.

MCB_Type_C.typical_use: Inductive loads — small motors, fluorescent lighting banks, LED driver panels with significant inrush, transformers.

MCB_Type_D.typical_use: Very high inrush — transformer primary circuits, motor-generator sets, large discharge lighting (HID), welding equipment.

gG_fuse_IEC_60269.typical_use: Submain protection, motor circuits (motor starter must provide overload protection separately), industrial distribution boards.
gG_fuse_IEC_60269.advantage_over_MCB: Lower let-through energy (I²t) at high fault levels — gives better cable protection near high-fault-level sources.

trip_characteristic_16A_gG._note: Typical 16A gG fuse — manufacturer curves required for precise design

aM_fuse_IEC_60269.type: aM = motor circuit — partial range fuse (short-circuit protection only, no overload protection)
aM_fuse_IEC_60269.note: aM fuses do NOT provide overload protection — they only interrupt short-circuit currents. Must always be used with a separate overload relay (motor starter). I2 factor = 1.6× In but the fuse will not blow at 1.6× In within 1 hour — there is no thermal overload capability.
aM_fuse_IEC_60269.typical_use: Motor circuit short-circuit protection, combined with contactor and overload relay in DOL starters.

motor_circuit_requirements.general: Motor circuits require overload protection in addition to short-circuit protection. The thermal trip threshold of the overload relay must be set to the motor's full-load current (FLC) from the nameplate.
motor_circuit_requirements.short_circuit_protection: gG fuses or Type D MCB (to handle starting current 5–7× FLC for DOL start).
motor_circuit_requirements.overload_relay_setting: Set to FLC from nameplate ±5%. Electronic relays allow more precise setting.
motor_circuit_requirements.combined_motor_starter: An MCB with motor-protective characteristic (Type K, or BS/IEC 60947-4 combination starter) provides both overload and short-circuit protection in one device.

worked_example._title: Cable sizing check for Fundamental Rule — 230V single-phase circuit
worked_example.scenario: 2.5mm² copper XLPE cable (single-phase, clipped direct — reference method C) supplying a 16A socket outlet circuit. Ze = 0.35Ω. Cable length 25m.

step1_design_current.Ib: 16A (maximum socket outlet load for a dedicated circuit)

step2_cable_capacity.It_from_table: 27A (2.5mm² copper XLPE, method C, from IEC 60364-5-52 Table B.52.4)

step4_ADS_check.R1_R2_for_2_5mm2: 0.0195 Ω/m (R1+R2 at 70°C for 2.5/1.5 cable) — use 2.5mm² conductor data: R at 90°C ≈ 2 × (0.00862 Ω/m) = 0.01724 Ω/m loop
step4_ADS_check._note: For simplified check: R1+R2 ≈ 18 mΩ/m for 2.5mm² outgoing + 2.5mm² CPC at 90°C

worked_example.result: 16A Type B MCB protecting 2.5mm² copper XLPE, Method C, 25m single-phase circuit at 230V — COMPLIES with all checks.
